title: Fixing the English
learningGoal: The learner will replace L1 loan-translations (transitive mechanical actions like 'open the light') with the correct splittable phrasal verbs, asserting sociolinguistic correctness.
instructions: Oh no! A robot has translated 3 Hindi sentences directly into English word-for-word. It sounds terrible! 1. I open the TV. 2. I close the light. 3. I wear my shoes (to get dressed). Rewrite these sentences replacing the bad verbs with natural Phrasal Verbs!

contentItems.text: CRITICAL RULE: Never use Open/Close for electronics. Use Turn on / Turn off. Never use 'Wear' as the action of taking a shoe and putting it on your foot. Use 'Put on'!

writingPrompt: Rewrite these 3 sentences: 1. I open the TV. 2. I close the light. 3. I wear my shoes.
promptGoal: Test independent substitution of L1 direct-translation errors with the appropriately matched phrasal verbs.

modelExampleOutputs.targetText: I turn on the TV. I turn off the light. I put on my shoes.
modelExampleOutputs.transliteration: आई टर्न ऑन द टीवी। आई टर्न ऑफ द लाइट। आई पुट ऑन माई शूज़।
modelExampleOutputs.baseTranslation: मैं टीवी चालू करता हूँ। मैं लाइट बंद करता हूँ। मैं अपने जूते पहनता हूँ।
modelExampleOutputs.annotation: Perfect score. The learner completely purged the direct L1 transitive translations ('open', 'close', 'wear') and substituted them with the correct phrasal units.

modelExampleOutputs.targetText: I open the TV. I turn the light. I put my shoes.
modelExampleOutputs.transliteration: आई ओपन द टीवी। आई टर्न द लाइट। आई पुट माई शूज़।
modelExampleOutputs.annotation: Critical failure. They did not attach the prepositions/adverbs (off, on), fundamentally altering the semantic meaning ('turn the light', 'put my shoes'), and failed to correct the first sentence entirely.

evaluationCriteria.description: Did the user definitively replace 'open' with 'turn on' and 'close' with 'turn off'?
Did the user replace the state-of-being verb 'wear' with the active phrasal verb 'put on'?

adminCorrectAnswerSet.sampleAnswer: I turn on the TV. I turn off the light. I put on my shoes.
acceptableAlternates: I turn the TV on. I turn the light off. I put my shoes on.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.grammarBaseline: Absolute zero tolerance for 'open/close' applied to machinery or electronics.

feedbackRules.onPass: Magnificent! You successfully destroyed the robot's bad English. You replaced the direct translations with native-sounding Phrasal Verbs!
feedbackRules.onFail: Please review your writing. Did you leave the words 'Open' or 'Close' in the sentence? English speakers NEVER 'Open' a TV. You MUST say 'Turn on'.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start! Double check Sentence 3. 'Wear' means the shoe is already on your foot. The action of taking the shoe and putting it on your foot is 'Put on'!

audioAssets.audioTranscript: Fix the sentences: I open the TV. I close the light. I wear my shoes.

hintText: Replace Open with Turn on. Replace Close with Turn off. Replace Wear with Put on.
commonMistakes: Leaving out the prepositions (e.g., 'I turn the TV', which makes no sense).
Leaving the word 'wear' in place due to stubborn L1 mapping.
